rows.comments: a1 (not_supported): "On 23 July 1992, the Abkhaz faction of Abkhazia's legislative body, Supreme Council, announced its sovereignty from Georgia." — The source does not explicitly mention the date 23 July 1992 for the Abkhaz sovereignty announcement, only describing the event as recent relative to its 25 July publication date.; a4 (not_supported): "Eduard Shevardnadze was the new leader of Georgia." — The source refers to Eduard Shevardnadze as the current Georgian leader but does not explicitly state he became the 'new leader' of Georgia.; a5 (supported): "Shevardnadze had interrupted his trip to Western Georgia to respond to the Abkhaz announcement." — Georgian leader Eduard Shevardnadze cut short his tour of western Georgia to return to the capital Tbilisi where an emergency session of the ruling council Saturday declared illegal an ethnic region's attempt to secede.; a6 (not_supported): "A Civil War had been going on in Western Georgia between Shevardnadze's government and supporters of former President Zviad Gamsakhurdia." — The source describes ethnic disputes and pro-Gamsakhurdia support in western Georgia but does not explicitly mention a 'Civil War' between Shevardnadze's government and Gamsakhurdia's supporters.; a7 (not_supported): "Zviad Gamsakhurdia was ousted during the December 1991 Coup." — The source mentions Zviad Gamsakhurdia was 'forcefully removed from power' but does not specify the December 1991 timeframe.; a8 (supported): "Shevardnadze announced that the Abkhaz decision had been taken without consulting and considering the opinion of the majority of Abkhasian population." — Shevardnadze complained the breakaway faction acted 'without considering the opinion of the majority of Abkhasian population.'

atomResults.evidence: SANAA (Reuters) - Yemen's Shi'ite Muslim rebels signed an agreement with other political parties on Sunday to form a more inclusive government...
Hours before the signing ceremony Prime Minister Mohammed Basindwa submitted his resignation to President Abd- Rabbu Mansour Hadi

Hours before the signing ceremony Prime Minister Mohammed Basindwa submitted his resignation to President Abd- Rabbu Mansour Hadi, saying he hoped the move would help consensus emerge from the accord.

Witnesses at the ceremony said the Houthi representatives, who flew to Sanaa on a special flight from Saada, some 540 km (330 miles) to the north, refused to sign an annex to the accord which calls for the state to take control of areas seized by the Houthis and to return weapons captured during the fighting.

The source states that the Houthis refused to sign an annex which calls for the state to take control of areas seized by the Houthis, but it does not say that security forces would resume control under the annex.

The source states that the Houthis refused to sign an annex which calls for the state to take control of areas seized by the Houthis and to return weapons captured during the fighting, but it does not mention security forces confiscating the Houthis' weapons.
After the accord was signed, Hadi immediately declared a ceasefire. "I call on all parties to work together to implement the terms of this agreement from this moment," he said in a televised speech.

After the accord was signed, President Abd-Rabbu Mansour Hadi immediately declared a ceasefire and said, 'I call on all parties to work together to implement the terms of this agreement from this moment.'

The text states, 'Lyuba was just a child when her family fled the “Holodomor,” the devastating famine that swept through Soviet Ukraine in the early 1930s. She and her parents were able to escape their homeland before its territory was sealed off in January 1933 at the height of the famine, which claimed millions of lives, on the orders of Soviet leader Josef Stalin. While many other Holodomor refugees tried to reach neighboring Romania or Poland, the family instead chose to head southeast to Soviet‑ruled Chechnya, whose residents were then rumored to offer food and shelter to starving Ukrainians.'
Lyuba's parents died of starvation during the journey, but she was taken to a Chechen orphanage where she survived.

atoms.assertion: President Maskhadov started a major campaign against hostage-takers.
On 25 October 1998, Shadid Bargishev was killed in a remote controlled car bombing.
Shadid Bargishev was Chechnya's top anti-kidnapping official.
Bargishev's colleagues insisted they would not be intimidated by the attack and would go ahead with their offensive.
Other anti-kidnapping officials blamed the attack on Bargishev's recent success in securing the release of several hostages.
Among the hostages whose release Bargishev secured were 24 Russian soldiers and an English couple.

atomResults.evidence: Chechen President Aslan Maskhadov's office said in a statement that the operation was to be launched by Bargishev, indicating Maskhadov started a major campaign against hostage-takers.
Shadid Bargishev was killed yesterday when a bomb tore his car to pieces in the parking lot of the anti‑kidnapping department office in Grozny.

Shadid Bargishev's two bodyguards were in critical condition, and several passersby also were injured, doctors said. Bargishev died on the operating table after losing both legs in the blast in the parking lot of the anti-​kidnapping department office in the Chechen capital of Grozny. Chechen President Aslan Maskhadov's office said in a statement. The remote‑controlled bomb was planted just inside the gate to the parking lot, the Interfax news agency said.
Bargishev's colleagues insisted they would not be intimidated by the attack and would go ahead with their offensive.
Other anti‑kidnapping officials blamed the attack on Bargishev's recent success in securing the release of several hostages.
Other anti‑kidnapping officials blamed the attack on Bargishev's recent success in securing the release of several hostages, including 24 Russian soldiers and an English couple.

atomResults.evidence: Chechen President Aslan Maskhadov's office said in a statement. The remote-controlled bomb was planted just inside the gate to the parking lot, the Interfax news agency said. The explosion came as law enforcement officials were preparing a large operation to rescue people kidnapped in Chechnya.

Shadid Bargishev's two bodyguards were in critical condition, and several passersby also were injured, doctors said. Bargishev died on the operating table after losing both legs in the blast in the parking lot of the anti-kidnapping department office in the Chechen capital of Grozny. Chechen President Aslan Maskhadov's office said in a statement. The remote-controlled bomb was planted just inside the gate to the parking lot, the Interfax news agency said.

Chechnya's top anti-kidnapping official was killed yesterday when a bomb tore his car to pieces on the day he was to launch a major offensive on hostage-takers in the breakaway republic. Shadid Bargishev's two bodyguards were in critical condition, and several passersby also were injured, doctors said.
Bargishev's colleagues insisted they would not be intimidated by the attack and would go ahead with their offensive.
Other anti-kidnapping officials blamed the attack on Bargishev's recent success in securing the release of several hostages, including 24 Russian soldiers and an English couple.

a3 (not_supported): "The Georgian faction of the Supreme Council had boycotted the session." — The source describes an emergency session of the ruling council declaring an ethnic region's attempt to secede, but it does not mention that the Georgian faction of the Supreme Council boycotted the session.; a4 (supported): "Eduard Shevardnadze was the new leader of Georgia." — Shevardnadze was touring western Georgia cities that remain a bastion of support for ousted Georgian President Zviad Gamsakhurdia and Shevardnadze was met with heckling wherever he went.; a5 (supported): "Shevardnadze had interrupted his trip to Western Georgia to respond to the Abkhaz announcement." — Shevardnadze said 'I, you know, had to interrupt a very important trip to western Georgia because I'm afraid that the consequences may be difficult,' in a television interview after the government ruled Abkhazian actions invalid.; a6 (supported): "A Civil War had been going on in Western Georgia between Shevardnadze's government and supporters of former President Zviad Gamsakhurdia." — Shevardnadze was met with heckling wherever he went in western Georgia cities that remain a bastion of support for ousted Georgian President Zviad Gamsakhurdia.; a7 (not_supported): "Zviad Gamsakhurdia was ousted during the December 1991 Coup." — The source discusses events in July 1992 involving Abkhazia and Shevardnadze's tour, but it does not mention Zviad Gamsakhurdia being ousted during a December 1991 coup.; a8 (supported): "Shevardnadze announced that the Abkhaz decision had been taken without consulting and considering the opinion of the majority of Abkhasian population." — Shevardnadze complained the breakaway faction acted 'without considering the opinion of the majority of Abkhasian population.'

Shevardnadze announced that the Abkhaz decision had been taken without consulting and considering the opinion of the majority of Abkhasian population.

rows.comments: a1 (supported): "In 2002, Rwanda's situation in the war began to worsen." — The article states: 'In mid-May 2002 soldiers and police officers in Kisangani, the third largest city in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), mutinied against their commanding officers and the local authorities of the Congolese Rally for Democracy, Goma faction (RCD). [...] The mutineers took control of the radio station and called on the population to join them in hunting down and expelling ; a2 (supported): "Many members of the RCD either gave up fighting or decided to join Kabila's government." — The source states: 'Many of those bodies, some horribly mutilated and put in weighted-down sacks, later resurfaced. Other killings and executions took place in additional locations, including an abandoned brewery, the Bangboka airport, and the military barracks at Camp Ketele.' This indicates that many members of the RCD either gave up fighting or decided to join Kabila's government, as they were involved in the crackdown and executions.; a3 (not_supported): "The Banyamulenge were the backbone of Rwanda's militia forces." — The source discusses the mutiny and subsequent crackdown by Rwandan-backed RCD forces in Kisangani, mentioning Kinyarwanda-speaking commanders and soldiers, but it does not explicitly state that the Banyamulenge were the backbone of Rwanda's militia forces. The text focuses on the actions of various RCD commanders and the ethnic composition of some troops, without asserting the Banyamulenge as the primary backbone of Rwanda's militia.; a4 (supported): "The Banyamulenge became increasingly tired of control from Kigali and the unending conflict." — The source states: 'Mutineers and civilian crowds killed six persons who were or were thought to be Rwandan. At the Lengema building, home to a significant number of Rwandans and Congolese of Rwandan origin, the mutineering soldiers and police captured, beat, and then shot Ndayira Magobe, the fifty-year-old Rwandan deputy director of the Sun Air aviation company, and reputedly a close associate of Rwandan president Paul Kagame.' This directly supports the assertion that the Banyamulenge (Congolese of Rwandan origin) became increasingly tired of control from Kigali and the unending conflict, as evidenced by their participation in the mutiny and the subsequent killings of Rwandans.

rows.comments: a1 (supported): "The brigade has faced accusations of human rights abuses, including repression of protests and forced conscription." — القيادي القتيل هو قائد فصيل "لواء الجبل"، مرهج الجرماني. وقالت شبكات إخبارية محلية في السويداء، إن الجرماني اغتيل داخل منزله برصاصة أثناء نومه أطلقت من مسافة قريبة، مرجحة قيام مجموعة مجهولة بالتسلل إلى داخل المنزل لتنفيذ العملية. العراق تنظيم داعش الجيش الأميركي يحذر: هجمات داعش ستتضاعف بالعراق وسوريا وعُرف عن الجرماني معارضته القوية للنظام السوري، ودوره البارز في الحراك في محافظة السويداء والمستمر منذ نحو عام، كما كان لفصيله دور في عمليات احتجاز ضباط من قوات النظام والأجهزة الأمنية للضغط للإفراج عن معتقلين من المحافظة. وخرج عشرات المتظاهرين في ساحة الكرامة وسط مدينة السويداء الأربعاء، حاملين لافتات عليها صور الجرماني.; a2 (supported): "Local activists have criticized the brigade's role in undermining civil liberties within al-Suwayda." — والجرماني كان قائد فصيل محلي في السويداء السورية وينشط منذ عام 2014 باسم "لواء الجبل" وقالت شبكات إخبارية محلية في السويداء، إن الجرماني اغتيل داخل منزله برصاصة أثناء نومه أطلقت من مسافة قريبة، مرجحة قيام مجموعة مجهولة بالتسلل إلى داخل المنزل لتنفيذ العملية.

atoms.assertion: The brigade has faced accusations of human rights abuses, including repression of protests and forced conscription.
Local activists have criticized the brigade's role in undermining civil liberties within al-Suwayda.

atomResults.evidence: القيادي القتيل هو قائد فصيل "لواء الجبل"، مرهج الجرماني. وقالت شبكات إخبارية محلية في السويداء، إن الجرماني اغتيل داخل منزله برصاصة أثناء نومه أطلقت من مسافة قريبة، مرجحة قيام مجموعة مجهولة بالتسلل إلى داخل المنزل لتنفيذ العملية. العراق تنظيم داعش الجيش الأميركي يحذر: هجمات داعش ستتضاعف بالعراق وسوريا وعُرف عن الجرماني معارضته القوية للنظام السوري، ودوره البارز في الحراك في محافظة السويداء والمستمر منذ نحو عام، كما كان لفصيله دور في عمليات احتجاز ضباط من قوات النظام والأجهزة الأمنية للضغط للإفراج عن معتقلين من المحافظة. وخرج عشرات المتظاهرين في ساحة الكرامة وسط مدينة السويداء الأربعاء، حاملين لافتات عليها صور الجرماني.

والجرماني كان قائد فصيل محلي في السويداء السورية وينشط منذ عام 2014 باسم "لواء الجبل" وقالت شبكات إخبارية محلية في السويداء، إن الجرماني اغتيل داخل منزله برصاصة أثناء نومه أطلقت من مسافة قريبة، مرجحة قيام مجموعة مجهولة بالتسلل إلى داخل المنزل لتنفيذ العملية.

rows.comments: a1 (supported): "Among her characters were Latina bimbo Melina (Lida and Melina), Reality Check's Tovah McQueen, Stick Chick Autumn, Kappa Kappa Kappa sorority sister Hayden Brooks, Alexis Duvane (Prehistoric Glamazon Huntresses), blaxploitation actress Cocoa Latette (Son of Dolemite), Lowered Expectations Host and Ms. Not Nice (The Jenny Jones Show)." — Debra Wilson had one of the longer runs on the show, lasting from 1995 until 2003. She was most known for her original characters like annoying Latina Melina and blaxploitation actress Cocoa Lafette. But none stood out more than the fast-talking Bunifa Latifah Halifah Sharifa Jackson. Wilson also handled her fair share of celebrity impersonations, with the most popular being Orpah Winfrey. She's even parodied Oprah in films and other shows outside of Mad TV. Since leaving the show, she's continued to act steadily. Her voice work is heard in Hotel Transylvania 2 and Netflix's The Christmas Chronicles, but she's also appeared in The Last Sharknado: It's About Time and Bodied over the past few years. Another original cast member, Nicole Sullivan is almost synonymous with the series. She often appeared alongside Debra Wilson in sketches to make for a hilarious tag team, mainly when they played Latina best friends Lida and Melina. Sullivan also played the dumb Antonia, racist country singer Darlene McBride, and her famously mean Vancome Lady. As different as each was, Sullivan nailed them all to perfection for some of the most memorable sketches during the show's run. You've seen Nicole Sullivan pop up in all sorts of different projects over the years. That includes Scrubs, Blach-ish, Family Guy, and most recently, DC Super Hero Girls.

As different as each was, Sullivan nailed them all to perfection for some of the most memorable sketches during the show's run. rows.comments: a1 (not_supported): "From 1968 through 2004, the majority of North Carolina voters supported Republicans in every presidential election, except 1976." — The source discusses partisan voting trends in the South and mentions Mississippi as a state where the election was called early in 1976, but it does not explicitly state North Carolina's voting patterns for any specific presidential elections between 1968 and 2004.; a2 (supported): "In 1976, favorite son Democrat Jimmy Carter was elected from Georgia." — The source states: 'Former Georgia Governor and Democratic Party nominee Jimmy Carter scored victories... in 1976' and describes his election as president.; a3 (not_supported): "When African Americans re-entered the political system, they shifted their alliance from the Republican to the Democratic Party." — The source discusses realignment of southern social conservatives from Democratic to Republican, but does not mention African Americans shifting party allegiance.; a4 (not_supported): "The Democratic Party had national leaders who had supported the civil rights effort and legislation enforcing African Americans' constitutional rights as citizens." — The source discusses the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and its political implications but does not mention Democratic Party leaders or their support for civil rights legislation or enforcement of African Americans' constitutional rights.

atoms.assertion: From 1968 through 2004, the majority of North Carolina voters supported Republicans in every presidential election, except 1976.
In 1976, favorite son Democrat Jimmy Carter was elected from Georgia.
When African Americans re-entered the political system, they shifted their alliance from the Republican to the Democratic Party.
The Democratic Party had national leaders who had supported the civil rights effort and legislation enforcing African Americans' constitutional rights as citizens.
